questions.text: A healthcare analytics company is building a custom training pipeline on Vertex AI to predict patient readmission risk. The training data is relatively small and stored in Cloud Storage. The training code uses scikit-learn and several other nonstandard Python packages that are not included in the default prebuilt containers. The data science team wants to minimize the startup time for each training job while making sure they can easily rerun the job multiple times during development. How should they configure the custom training job?

options.text: Use the scikit-learn prebuilt container, download the training data from Cloud Storage at runtime, and install the required dependencies using pip within the training script
options.explanation:
The correct solution is to create a custom container with all dependencies already included and store the dataset in Cloud Storage. This keeps the container lightweight while avoiding runtime dependency installation, which can significantly delay startup. Reading a small dataset from Cloud Storage has negligible impact on performance and allows data updates without rebuilding the container. This approach minimizes startup time, simplifies iteration, and keeps both data and code management flexible.

The other options introduce unnecessary delays or complexity. Installing dependencies at runtime slows job startup and can make results inconsistent if package versions change. Embedding the dataset inside the container increases image size, making it slower to pull and less flexible to update data without rebuilding the image. Using a prebuilt container without modification forces you to install dependencies during execution, which is slower than having them pre-installed. Storing data in Cloud Storage and pulling it at runtime is faster and more scalable than bundling it directly into the container for this scenario.

questions.text: You are developing a prototype recommendation system in a managed Vertex AI Workbench notebook. For data manipulation, you decide to try the Dask library so you can handle larger datasets more efficiently. You want to install it right away and begin experimenting without stopping your current notebook session. What should you do?

options.text: Install the Dask library from a terminal session by running pip install dask
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to run !pip install dask --user inside a Jupyter cell. This ensures that the library is installed into the environment used by the active kernel, so you can start importing and using it immediately without restarting the kernel or rebuilding the environment. It keeps the workflow fast and contained within the current managed Vertex AI Workbench session.

The other approaches are slower or unrelated to the immediate goal. Installing from a terminal is possible but may require restarting the notebook kernel, which interrupts the session. Creating a new environment with Dask pre-installed involves extra setup steps that are unnecessary for quick experimentation. Running a Dataproc job to use Dask shifts the workflow to a distributed processing cluster, which is excessive for a rapid prototype within the notebook.

questions.text: Your team has built a fraud detection model using Vertex AI Prediction, and it is running in production. The automated training pipeline has produced a new version of the model that passed unit tests and performance benchmarks. You want to evaluate this new model in a way that ensures uninterrupted fraud detection for customers and avoids any degradation in service during the evaluation period. What should you do?

options.text: Deploy the current model version and the new model version on Google Kubernetes Engine with Istio, using it to send a small percentage of live traffic to the new version before gradually increasing it
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to deploy the new model in a separate Vertex AI endpoint and run it alongside the current model using shadow deployment testing. This means both models receive identical input requests, but only the outputs from the current production model are used for actual customer-facing fraud decisions. The outputs from the new model are logged for comparison and evaluation against the current model, allowing you to analyze real-world performance without any risk to users. This approach ensures zero downtime, eliminates the possibility of degraded fraud detection during the trial period, and provides realistic performance data before deciding to replace the production model.

The other options introduce unnecessary risk because they expose real users to a model that has not yet been tested in production conditions. Traffic splitting in Vertex AI Prediction or Istio-based canary releases send live traffic to the new model, meaning some users will receive results from an unproven system. Even if the percentage is small, there is still a chance of errors or performance drops. Seldon Core with a multi-armed bandit strategy dynamically routes traffic between models, which can optimize over time but still exposes customers to potential errors and requires more complex infrastructure changes that could cause service interruptions. These methods fail to guarantee a fully controlled evaluation without impacting production service quality.

questions.text:
An online home goods store is using Recommendations AI to personalize product suggestions for shoppers. The business goal is to increase the total value of items purchased in a single transaction. You have already set up the data ingestion process and are ready to configure the recommendation type. The team asks for a solution that aligns with revenue growth rather than general user engagement.

Which recommendation type should you use?

options.text: Use placeholder product data to test the setup and see if the model returns results
options.explanation:
The ‘Frequently Bought Together’ recommendation type is designed to increase transaction size by suggesting items that are commonly purchased together. It directly supports a revenue objective by encouraging customers to add complementary products to their cart.

The ‘Other Products You May Like’ type is useful for increasing interaction, but it focuses on engagement and does not directly optimize for total purchase value. Importing user event data first may help model training but does not address the recommendation strategy. Using placeholder product data compromises the model’s reliability and violates best practices for data integrity. To increase revenue, the recommendation type must align with purchasing behavior rather than browsing behavior.

questions.text: You are building a churn prediction model for a subscription streaming service. Your dataset includes a column for subscription type, which has several possible categories. You split the data into training and validation sets, then apply one-hot encoding to the subscription type column in the training data. You notice that one subscription type present in the training data is missing from the validation data. What should you do?

options.text: Remove the subscription type column entirely from the validation data
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to ensure that the validation data is encoded with the same category mapping as the training data, even if a category does not appear in the validation set. This keeps the feature space consistent and prevents the model from misinterpreting the inputs. A category missing in the validation data should still have its column present with values set to zero. This is a standard step in preprocessing pipelines to ensure compatibility between training and validation phases.

The other options are incorrect because label encoding changes the feature representation and can distort relationships for models that expect one-hot encoded inputs. Merging categories based on similarity changes the problem definition and could introduce bias. Removing the entire feature from the validation data breaks alignment with the training set, leading to inconsistent input dimensions and invalid evaluation results.

questions.text: Your company develops a machine learning model on Vertex AI to analyze sensitive healthcare records. The training data is stored in an internal API endpoint that is only accessible within your organization’s private network. Regulatory policy requires that no public endpoints are exposed during training and that data exfiltration risks are strictly controlled. You need to configure Vertex AI training so that the data is accessed securely within GCP boundaries while meeting these requirements. What should you do?

options.text: Deploy a Cloud Run service to act as a gateway to the API, restricting access with IAM so only the training job can send requests
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to place Vertex AI inside a VPC Service Controls service perimeter and configure VPC Service Controls with peering. This ensures that access to the internal API endpoint occurs entirely within GCP’s controlled network boundaries, preventing the exposure of public endpoints and mitigating the risk of data leaving the defined perimeter. The service perimeter enforces security policies for sensitive workloads and aligns with strict compliance requirements, making it possible to securely retrieve the data during training without broadening the attack surface.

The other options do not meet the same level of protection. VPC Peering without VPC Service Controls does not prevent potential data exfiltration since there is no perimeter enforcement. A Cloud Run gateway still exposes a public endpoint, which fails the requirement to avoid public access entirely. Staging the data in Cloud Storage before training increases the risk of exposure because the bucket access is not automatically limited by a network boundary, and object-level IAM alone cannot ensure the same level of isolation as VPC Service Controls.

questions.text: A logistics company wants to run a pipeline that runs regularly every two weeks and predicts which delivery routes are most likely to experience delays two weeks into the future. The predictions will be used by the operations team to adjust driver schedules. The historical GPS and delivery time data is stored in Cloud Storage. You have trained a gradient boosting model in Vertex AI Workbench and now need to automate the entire process from data access through prediction output, while keeping costs low and minimizing code maintenance. The output should be stored in a secure location accessible to the operations team. What should you do?

options.text: Set up a scheduled pipeline in Vertex AI Pipelines that accesses data from Cloud Storage, runs training and batch prediction in Vertex AI, and writes the prediction output to a secure Cloud Storage bucket for the operations team
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to use Vertex AI Pipelines integrated with Cloud Storage to run training and batch prediction, with the output stored in a secure Cloud Storage bucket. This setup supports the biweekly schedule, minimizes manual intervention, and avoids unnecessary infrastructure management. The operations team can access the predictions directly from Cloud Storage without having to work inside the ML environment.

The other options have drawbacks. Cloud Composer with BigQuery ML adds complexity and cost since it requires maintaining a Composer environment and using a tool that is not optimized for this particular training setup. Running a scheduled notebook in Vertex AI Workbench requires manual environment updates and more code maintenance. Cloud Composer with an email output is both costly to run continuously and risky from a security standpoint, since sending prediction results containing operational data by email is not a recommended secure data sharing method.

questions.text: You are developing a machine learning system for a job application platform. Users upload resumes, and the system filters out submissions that do not meet formatting standards. If an invalid resume is accepted, it causes problems downstream in the processing pipeline. You need to reduce the chances that a poorly formatted resume is mistakenly accepted. What is the most appropriate approach?

options.text: Use Vertex AI Workbench to train a model with three times more examples of improperly formatted resumes than properly formatted ones
options.explanation:
To reduce false positives (resumes incorrectly marked as valid) the model should be more sensitive to invalid formatting. Training with an imbalanced dataset where invalid examples dominate helps the model focus on recognizing those problematic cases. Vertex AI Workbench gives full control over preprocessing and model development, making it suitable for this type of targeted tuning.

Tuning for precision using AutoML would not allow enough control over data imbalance and may still miss the goal if you can’t weight error types effectively. Overrepresenting valid resumes risks a model that is biased toward accepting them, increasing false positives. Tuning for recall using AutoML prioritizes minimizing false negatives, which is not the primary concern in this case.

The pipeline retrieves product interaction data from BigQuery, processes it, applies several feature transformation steps, trains the model, evaluates its performance, and then uploads the model artifact to Cloud Storage.

options.text: Enable caching for all steps in the Vertex AI pipeline
options.explanation:

When iterating on only certain parts of the workflow, this significantly reduces total execution time without increasing cost. Vertex AI Pipelines supports step caching natively, making it a straightforward optimization that fits well into iterative model development.

Moving transformations to BigQuery can shift the workload but will not meaningfully shorten overall runtime unless that stage is the main bottleneck. questions.text: You are working with a healthcare analytics team that is building an ML model to predict patient readmission risk. The other options fail to meet the balance between privacy and usability. Random number replacement destroys relationships in the data, which can significantly harm model accuracy. AES-256 encryption with a salt does not preserve the original format or value relationships, which can break downstream feature processing. Removing sensitive columns entirely violates the requirement that all fields are needed for prediction, which would compromise the model’s performance.

questions.text: A large-scale e-commerce platform maintains personalized search ranking models built with TensorFlow. The models use billions of clickstream events and over 80,000 unique categorical product IDs. The current training runs on a small GPU cluster, but as the dataset grows, training time has increased significantly. The team decides to migrate training to Google Cloud to improve scalability and performance for these models. You need to choose the most suitable approach for handling the large embedding tables efficiently while minimizing training time. What should you do?

options.text: Run the training on an autoscaling Vertex AI Training job using CPUs in a GKE cluster
options.explanation:
TPU VMs with Pod slices are designed for high-throughput workloads like large-scale recommendation systems. The TPUEmbedding API is optimized for efficiently handling very large embedding tables, reducing communication overhead and making full use of TPU architecture. This combination accelerates training on datasets with billions of interactions and tens of thousands of categorical features, delivering significantly lower training time compared to CPU or even large GPU configurations. The v4 generation TPUs also provide improved scaling for embedding-heavy deep learning workloads.

The other options are not optimal for this use case. Running on A100 GPUs with standard embedding lookups can work for smaller embedding tables but is less efficient at scale due to memory bandwidth and lookup overhead. BigQuery ML does not support training deep learning recommendation models with large embedding workflows, and matrix factorization alone cannot capture the complex feature interactions present in this dataset. Using CPUs in an autoscaling GKE cluster does not provide enough computational throughput for large deep learning models, making training much slower compared to TPU-based approaches.

questions.text:
You are training a recommendation model using BigQuery and Vertex AI Pipelines. Your input dataset contains user interactions with products. To create separate datasets for training and evaluation, you write two SQL queries using RAND():

CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE `company.analytics.train_set` AS
SELECT * FROM `company.analytics.user_events`
WHERE RAND() < 0.9;


CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE `company.analytics.eval_set` AS
SELECT * FROM `company.analytics.user_events`
WHERE RAND() < 0.3;

After training the model, your evaluation metrics look strong. But once you deploy the model to predict for new users, click-through rates are much lower than expected. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The evaluation set contains too few records to represent real user behavior
options.explanation:
The validation dataset contains records that are also in the training dataset because the same RAND() condition is reused with different thresholds. A record that gets a value below 0.3 will appear in both tables, making the model look more accurate than it really is.

Time-based splits can sometimes help but aren't required here and wouldn't explain the sharp drop in real-world performance.

Too few records in the evaluation set might cause instability in metrics but not a consistent drop post-deployment.

Overfitting to non-predictive features can harm performance but doesn’t explain why validation looked fine while real-world performance dropped.

The overlap between training and validation is what causes inflated validation scores that don’t hold up in production.

questions.text: Your team has built a pipeline that cleans customer transaction data and stores the output in a Cloud Storage bucket. You want to trigger model retraining as soon as new data arrives. You use Kubeflow Pipelines on Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) for training, and you want to incorporate this into a CI/CD workflow that minimizes manual intervention and component complexity. What should you do?

options.text: Use Cloud Scheduler to check Cloud Storage at regular intervals. If new data is found, invoke the training job on GKE.
options.explanation:
The best solution is to use a Cloud Storage event trigger to launch a Cloud Run Function. Cloud Storage natively supports event notifications to Cloud Run Functions when objects are added to a bucket. This creates a lightweight, serverless, and event-driven workflow. The Cloud Run Function can then initiate the training job on your Kubeflow Pipelines setup running on GKE. This removes the need for intermediary services like Pub/Sub or polling mechanisms and integrates cleanly into a CI/CD setup for automated retraining.

The Dataflow approach adds unnecessary complexity and is not inherently event-driven. Polling via App Engine wastes resources and introduces latency. Cloud Scheduler introduces delay and adds complexity without offering real-time triggering. Direct Cloud Storage triggers are now the simplest and most efficient pattern for this use case.

questions.text: A logistics company stores unstructured driver delivery logs in Cloud Storage. These logs contain both operational metrics and customer PII (Personally Identifiable Information) such as names, phone numbers, and addresses. You plan to process the logs into a structured format for downstream analytics in BigQuery. Company policy mandates that personally identifiable information must not leave Cloud Storage in identifiable form. You need a scalable approach that keeps the analytics data useful while meeting the policy requirements. What should you do?

options.text: Use the DLP API to de-identify the PII before loading the structured output into BigQuery
options.explanation:
The DLP API can detect and de-identify personally identifiable information in files stored in Cloud Storage before they are moved to other systems. De-identification methods such as masking, tokenization, or generalization ensure the PII is no longer directly linked to individuals while preserving the analytical value of the data. This allows you to transform the unstructured logs into structured form and load them into BigQuery without violating the policy on storing identifiable PII.

The other options fail to satisfy the requirement or introduce avoidable complexity. Row-level security in BigQuery controls access but does not remove the PII, so the data would still exist in identifiable form in BigQuery. Storing the raw logs in a restricted dataset still moves identifiable PII out of Cloud Storage, which breaks policy. Writing a Cloud Run Functions service to manually remove PII is less scalable, harder to maintain, and more error-prone than using the built-in detection and de-identification features of the DLP API.

options.text: Use the Cloud Speech-to-Text API and analyze sentiment at the individual word level
options.explanation:
Using the Cloud Speech-to-Text API removes vocal features that may correlate with demographic traits, helping reduce unintended bias. Analyzing full sentences captures the meaning and emotional tone more accurately than word-level analysis.

Raw acoustic features can introduce bias, since they are linked to personal identity characteristics.

questions.text:
Your team is running a model validation pipeline in Vertex AI Pipelines. The validation step uses TensorFlow Model Analysis (TFMA) and currently runs on a local direct runner. When evaluating a recent large dataset, the job fails due to memory exhaustion. You want to keep the same evaluation quality and avoid making major architectural changes, while also reducing the risk of hitting memory limits in the future.

What should you do?

options.text: Split the evaluation dataset into smaller batches and run multiple evaluation jobs sequentially
options.explanation:
Running the evaluation step on Dataflow allows the TFMA job to scale horizontally and handle large datasets without exhausting memory on a single worker. Dataflow uses Apache Beam under the hood, so specifying the Dataflow runner shifts execution to a managed distributed environment. This change minimizes infrastructure adjustments while retaining the same evaluation logic and metrics. It also helps prevent future failures when datasets grow in size.

Increasing Kubernetes pod memory could help temporarily but does not offer the same scalability benefits and could require larger infrastructure costs over time. Splitting the evaluation dataset into smaller batches changes how metrics are computed and could reduce statistical reliability. Exporting to Cloud Storage and aggregating in BigQuery would require changing the evaluation process entirely, which does not meet the requirement of keeping evaluation quality unchanged.

questions.text:
An energy monitoring platform needs to track thousands of power grid sensors and detect unusual behavior as it happens. Sensor data is published continuously through Pub/Sub. You are building a system that must handle the stream, run an ML model to identify anomalies, and store the results so the operations team can explore trends and build dashboards. The solution must scale automatically and require minimal infrastructure management.

Which combination of services should you use?

options.text: Dataflow, Vertex AI, BigQuery
options.explanation:
This combination is the best fit for a system that processes streaming sensor data, performs machine learning inference, and supports exploration and visualization. Dataflow handles the continuous ingestion and transformation of data from Pub/Sub. Vertex AI can host a custom anomaly detection model and serve predictions in real time. BigQuery is well suited for storing the results because it allows easy aggregation, trend analysis, and integration with dashboarding tools.

Bigtable is often used with sensor data because it supports high-throughput writes and time series workloads. However, it is optimized for low-latency lookups and operational access patterns, not for analytical tasks. It does not support native querying for metrics like averages or counts across many devices and time windows. For this use case, where operations teams need to explore patterns and visualize insights, BigQuery is a better fit.

The other options fall short in different ways. Cloud Storage is not intended for structured querying. AutoML does not currently offer anomaly detection. Cloud Run Functions are not designed for sustained, high-volume stream processing. Firestore is a document database and does not scale well for analytical workloads. Dataproc adds unnecessary complexity for a streaming pipeline and is better suited to batch jobs, and does not integrate as natively with PubSub.

questions.text: A video streaming analytics company is building a recommendation model in TensorFlow to personalize content suggestions. options.text: Create a Vertex AI custom training job with multiple worker pools that include GPU accelerators and use tf.distribute.MultiWorkerMirroredStrategy for distribution
options.explanation:
The best choice is to run a Vertex AI custom training job with GPU-enabled worker pools and tf.distribute.MultiWorkerMirroredStrategy. This approach provides synchronous distributed training across multiple workers with minimal code modifications and allows Vertex AI to manage TF_CONFIG and other cluster setup steps automatically. It is well-suited for computationally heavy recommendation model training and does not require specialized hardware-specific changes like TPUs.

The parameter server strategy with mixed machine types introduces more complexity without clear benefits for this workload. TPU-based training requires significant code adjustments for TPUStrategy compatibility and may not align with the current training code. options.text: Develop a regression model using scikit-learn and train it on Vertex AI Training

questions.text:
A renewable energy company built a Vertex AI AutoML model to predict the next day’s solar farm output using weather forecasts, historical production data, and maintenance schedules. The model is deployed to production and receives prediction requests daily. Data availability varies depending on weather station reporting, so some days provide fewer records than others. The team is concerned that shifts in seasonal sunlight patterns and changes in weather prediction accuracy could alter how features contribute to predictions. They want to detect these changes reliably and are not constrained by monitoring costs.

What should you do?

options.text: Create a feature attribution drift monitoring job with a sampling rate of 0.1 and a monthly monitoring frequency
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to use feature attribution drift monitoring with a sampling rate higher than 0.5 and a weekly frequency. A higher sampling rate ensures that most of the available data is included in the monitoring process, which is important when incoming records may be sparse on certain days. This setup focuses on detecting shifts in how features influence predictions, such as changes in the relationship between forecasted cloud cover and actual solar output. Weekly monitoring provides timely detection while still smoothing out short-term fluctuations that could appear in sparse datasets.

The other options are less effective. Feature drift monitoring only identifies distribution changes, not shifts in predictive contribution, which is the key concern here. Monthly monitoring delays detection too much for operational decision-making. A sampling rate of 0.5 or lower risks missing important changes in attribution patterns, especially when the dataset for a given period is small. Lower sampling rates are more relevant when controlling costs, which is not a constraint in this scenario.

questions.text: You work for a credit card company that uses machine learning to estimate fraud risk for incoming transactions. The average model output is a fraud score of 0.1, but a recent transaction triggered a much higher score of 0.82. The transaction came from a known device in a familiar location but involved an unusually large amount. You want to explain this specific score and how it differs from the average, using a method available in Vertex Explainable AI. What should you do?

options.text: Configure sampled Shapley explanations in Vertex Explainable AI
options.explanation:
Sampled Shapley explanations work by attributing the prediction difference from the baseline to each feature. This approach is supported in Vertex Explainable AI and is especially useful for understanding individual predictions from models like ensembles or gradient-boosted trees. It gives a breakdown of how each input contributed to the high fraud score compared to the average.

Manually subtracting feature effects is not meaningful without model internals. Integrated gradients are useful only for differentiable models such as neural networks and are not applicable to trees or ensembles. Gain-based feature importances help with global insight but cannot isolate what caused a single prediction to deviate sharply from the mean.

questions.text: You are building a machine learning model for a job-matching platform that recommends open positions to applicants. The dataset will include user activity from your production system. You want to ensure the model does not introduce or amplify bias against certain demographic groups. Which actions should you take? (Choose two.)

options.text: Collect only data from applicants who were hired in the past year
options.explanation:
Stratified sampling on production data ensures that the training dataset both reflects the real-world inputs the model will see and includes adequate representation of underrepresented groups. Using production data captures the current patterns, behavior trends, and demographic distributions present in the live environment, which helps the model generalize effectively when deployed. The stratification step makes sure that groups that naturally appear less often in the production stream are still included in sufficient numbers for training. This combination reduces the risk of biased performance while keeping the dataset relevant to current use cases. Fairness testing across sensitive categories then provides a quantitative way to detect and address disparities in model predictions, which is an important step before deployment.

The other options are either incomplete or introduce bias. Simply excluding demographic attributes does not prevent bias if correlated features remain in the dataset. Limiting the data to groups with the highest activity skews the dataset toward majority groups, omitting others with valuable patterns. Using only data from previously hired applicants creates historical bias, as past hiring decisions may have been influenced by biased practices. These approaches fail to ensure balanced representation and fairness in the model.

questions.text: You are building a machine learning model to estimate delivery times for a logistics platform. One of the key features is the average historical traffic delay for each route. While exploring the data, you find that this feature is frequently missing, but its values tend to follow a predictable pattern based on time of day, region, and distance. All rows in your dataset represent recent deliveries and must be retained. How should you handle the missing values in this feature?

options.text: Create a synthetic variable that encodes traffic congestion patterns and ignore the original field
options.explanation: Linear regression is a suitable method for estimating missing values when the feature in question is important and its values correlate with other available features. In this case, traffic delay varies with time, region, and distance, making it possible to predict missing values using those inputs. This helps preserve the information in the original feature while avoiding the loss of any rows. Dropping rows would reduce the size and diversity of your dataset, which can negatively affect model performance. Replacing missing values with a single constant like the mean ignores meaningful variation and can introduce bias. Creating a new feature might be useful in some contexts, but it does not solve the problem of preserving an existing predictor that is still relevant. Regression imputation strikes a good balance by filling in the missing values with informed estimates based on observed patterns in the data. It allows you to retain every row while maintaining the integrity of your predictive features.

Regression imputation strikes a good balance by filling in the missing values with informed estimates based on observed patterns in the data. It allows you to retain every row while maintaining the integrity of your predictive features.

questions.text: A financial analytics firm is training a deep learning model in TensorFlow Keras to detect anomalies in large-scale transaction data. The training run is expected to last several days and requires high-precision float32 arithmetic. The model is built entirely with built-in TensorFlow operations. The engineering team wants to update the code to use tf.distribute.Strategy for distributed training and run the job on Compute Engine to reduce total training time. You need to configure the training environment to allow horizontal scaling while supporting efficient data distribution. What should you do?

options.text: Create a TPU virtual machine, scale up the machine type until performance plateaus, add TPU initialization code, define a TPUStrategy in TensorFlow, and adjust batch size based on the number of TPU cores
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to use an instance group with GPU-enabled machines and MultiWorkerMirroredStrategy, configured with TF_CONFIG for distributed execution and data autosharding. This setup supports high-precision float32 workloads while allowing horizontal scaling across multiple workers. By starting with a smaller machine type and scaling until gains level off, you can find the optimal balance between compute resources and execution time. MultiWorkerMirroredStrategy provides synchronous training across multiple GPU-equipped instances, and data autosharding helps distribute the dataset efficiently to each worker.

The other options are less appropriate. A single-machine MirroredStrategy limits scaling potential since it only uses GPUs on one instance, which can become a bottleneck for multi-day training. TPU-based options are not well suited here because TPUs are optimized for lower-precision formats like bfloat16 and may not deliver the same efficiency for float32 workloads. Both TPU choices also require additional initialization and configuration steps that do not align with the needs of a high-precision model built exclusively with TensorFlow operations for GPU execution.

questions.text: A wildlife conservation group maintains a public website where volunteers can share photos from motion-sensor cameras placed in forests. Around 15 photos are uploaded each day. questions.text: A financial services company is designing an automated machine learning platform for fraud detection. The platform will run many training and evaluation pipelines each month, producing large binary model files and a variety of metadata such as parameters, execution logs, and lineage information. The source code for the models will also need to be version controlled. You need to choose storage solutions for each type of artifact to ensure maintainability and efficient retrieval. How should you store these artifacts?

options.text: Store parameters in Firestore, place the models’ source code in GitLab, and save the models’ binaries in GitHub
options.explanation:
Vertex ML Metadata is the most suitable place for storing parameters and related pipeline metadata because it is purpose-built for experiment tracking, artifact lineage, and pipeline execution history. Source code should be kept in a version control system such as GitLab or GitHub, which is designed for collaborative development and change tracking. Cloud Storage is the best choice for storing large binary artifacts such as trained models because it is optimized for scalable and cost-effective storage of large files while integrating easily with Vertex AI workflows. Using this separation of tools ensures that each component is stored in a service designed for that specific type of data.

The other options introduce inefficiencies or scalability problems. BigQuery is designed for analytical queries, not pipeline metadata tracking, and would not integrate as effectively with Vertex AI pipelines. Cloud SQL can store structured data but lacks the direct pipeline integration and artifact tracking capabilities of Vertex ML Metadata. Artifact Registry is optimized for container and package storage rather than large ML model binaries. Firestore is a NoSQL document database and is not intended for high-volume metadata queries or large binary storage. Storing binaries in GitHub is inefficient and can cause repository bloat, which impacts performance and usability.

questions.text: Your team is creating a credit risk scoring service that returns a probability value for each loan application as it is submitted. The model relies on features stored in BigQuery, which are refreshed every 15 minutes by a scheduled query. The service must respond quickly, scale automatically with demand, and require as little operational upkeep as possible. What should you do?

options.text: Configure a Cloud Run Function to query BigQuery for features during each prediction request and send them to a Vertex AI model endpoint
options.explanation:
The most effective choice is to configure Vertex AI Feature Store to automatically load features from BigQuery and serve them directly to the model, then deploy the model on a custom Vertex AI endpoint with automatic scaling. This design keeps predictions fast by avoiding repeated queries to BigQuery during inference and reduces the need for manual updates to the feature pipeline. It also allows the service to adjust to changing traffic levels without requiring infrastructure management, ensuring low latency and minimal operational work.

The other approaches have trade-offs that make them less suitable here. Querying BigQuery on every request slows down response times and adds unnecessary query costs. Using Memorystore with Kubernetes introduces extra infrastructure to manage and does not offer the same native connection with Vertex AI services. Pushing features to Feature Store but serving the model from a Compute Engine VM limits elasticity and adds maintenance overhead. These factors make them more complex and less efficient for a low-maintenance, real-time scoring system.

questions.text:
A logistics company is developing an internal assistant to summarize and annotate customer delivery feedback before support agents review it. The assistant needs to identify the type of item mentioned (such as perishable or fragile), estimate the likely time needed to resolve the issue, and determine the sentiment of the review. The final summary should be in plain language for internal teams.

The system uses the following approach:

First, customer feedback is published to a Pub/Sub topic. Then, Cloud Run service consumes the messages and calls three separate ML endpoints:

  • one to classify the item type
  • one to predict resolution time
  • one to analyze sentiment

The summarized output is displayed on an internal dashboard.

Which services should the Cloud Run service invoke for each endpoint?

options.text: Vertex AI for item classification, Vertex AI for time estimation, Cloud Natural Language API for sentiment analysis
options.explanation:
Vertex AI is the right choice for item classification and resolution time estimation because both tasks depend on the company’s specific data and categories. These are not general problems that can be handled well by pre-trained APIs. Vertex AI supports training and serving custom models that understand business-specific logic.

For sentiment analysis, the Cloud Natural Language API is a better option. It is a pre-trained service that works well for general text sentiment and does not require any custom training or infrastructure.

The options using AutoML for sentiment analysis involve more effort than necessary. AutoML can support text tasks, but you would need to train and host your own model. That adds complexity for a task that the Cloud Natural Language API already handles accurately out of the box.

The option that uses the Cloud Natural Language API for item classification is not appropriate because the API is not designed to detect custom item types like fragile or perishable. That kind of classification needs a model trained on specific data. The Cloud Vision API is also not useful here because it is for image content, not text.

Using the right combination of custom models for specific logic and pre-trained services for common tasks helps you build a simpler and more effective system.

questions.text: You are collaborating with a healthcare analytics team to train large-scale deep learning models for medical image classification in TensorFlow. The models require frequent iteration and debugging during development. You want to keep debugging straightforward while also speeding up training by using multiple devices. How should you set up your training environment?

options.text: Deploy an n1-standard-8 VM with 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs. SSH into the VM and use ParameterServerStrategy to train the model
options.explanation:
The correct setup uses a single VM with multiple GPUs and MirroredStrategy. This approach enables synchronous distributed training across GPUs within a single machine while keeping the code and environment contained in one VM, which makes debugging simpler and more straightforward.

MirroredStrategy is specifically designed for single-machine, multi-GPU setups. It creates mirror copies of model variables on each GPU and uses all-reduce operations to synchronize gradients across devices. This provides excellent performance scaling while maintaining the simplicity of working within a single compute environment.

Since the model is developed in TensorFlow and will be iterated on frequently, using GPUs in this configuration strikes the optimal balance between faster training performance and manageable debugging complexity. Developers can easily SSH into the VM, use familiar debugging tools, and iterate quickly on model architecture and hyperparameters.

The TPU options are incorrect because TPU environments are less flexible for step-by-step debugging during active development, and the setup can be more complex for interactive model iteration. Using ParameterServerStrategy on a single VM is not optimal here, as it is designed for larger, multi-node distributed systems and adds unnecessary configuration overhead without benefits at this scale. A TPU node accessed via Cloud Shell would also significantly limit debugging flexibility compared to working directly in a VM with GPUs.

The Cloud Natural Language API cannot support custom categories because it only detects predefined entities. Vertex AI built-in containers allow custom modeling but involve more setup and configuration. Keyword matching would be brittle and lacks the generalization and accuracy of a trained model. AutoML allows training directly on labeled text examples and is suited for this use case.

questions.text: You are building a customer support automation system that classifies incoming email messages into different request categories. Your training data contains millions of emails and a vocabulary of over 80,000 unique words. You plan to use a recurrent neural network to process the emails at the word level. How should you encode the input words before feeding them into the model?

options.text: Assign each word a unique numeric index and use the index values as direct input into the RNN
options.explanation:
Pre-trained word embeddings capture relationships between words based on how they appear in large text corpora. These embeddings map each word to a dense vector, which provides meaningful structure for downstream models like RNNs. This is especially important when dealing with a large vocabulary, as it allows the model to generalize across similar terms.

One-hot vectors are too memory-intensive for large vocabularies and contain no relational information. Raw index values treat words as unrelated and imply an ordinal relationship that does not exist. Frequency counts reflect how common a word is but do not capture word meaning or context, which is crucial for classification tasks involving free-text input.
